Good to see some familiar faces and good firms at the expo today, but what a waste of time for the average prop firm trader attending.

Firms paid 20k to 60k for a stand, and most people paid on top just to get through the door... how much money did the event organisers make?

For something so expensive, it was badly organised, poorly executed, and offering nothing of real value beyond somewhere to socialise..

These events are a net negative on the space and a leech on money that could go toward things that actually help people. Networking and free merch have their place... but not at this price point or scale.

I have nothing against any firm that attended, in fact, most of them I like a lot and are good firms, but I think it's time to just talk about the elephant in the room...

Passing a challenge account or maintaining a funded account, which is more difficult?

I say maintaining a funded account, you?

"put me on bro"

For the next 6 months, you’re going to spend more time staring at charts than with your friends and family. You’re going to backtest setups until your eyes burn, journal every loss, and sit on your hands when there’s nothing to take. You’re going to wake up before the session, check the news, and go to sleep replaying the trades you got wrong. You’re going to blow a demo, then fail your first prop firm challenge, then your second, then question whether you’re even built for this. Then, after all that, you’re going to pass an evaluation and risk a funded account on a setup you’ve seen a thousand times, knowing one bad day can breach it. You’re going to watch other people post payouts while you’re down on the month, grinding through a drawdown nobody sees. And after all of that screen time, all of that discipline, all of those reset fees, you’re still only a fraction of the way to becoming consistently profitable.

THE LAST SUPPORT 🏁

If London gives me above 60% probability…

I stop guessing direction.
I stop trading both sides.

Last week’s London session performance from 2am showed exactly why:

Trade ONLY towards the more probable side.
Use Asia session as the map.
Wait for iFVG.
Execute mechanically.
Target 2R.

That’s it.

When the Intelligent Balance indicator shows London bias above 60%, I am not interested in fighting it.

60%+ LONGS?
I look for longs only.
60%+ SHORTS?
I look for shorts only.

Below 60%?
That can be barcode/choppy price action - and sometimes the best trade is no trade.

The biggest mistake most traders make during London session is trying to predict every candle.

My approach is different:
Asia session builds the range.
London session gives the probability.
iFVG gives the entry.
2R is the mechanical target.

No emotional guessing.
No forcing trades both ways.
No “maybe it reverses here”.
Just trading in the direction where price has a higher probability of delivery.

Direction first.
Confirmation second.
Execution third.

Would you take every London iFVG only when the bias is above 60%?

Our top affiliates made more from one referral link than most creators make from a brand deal.

That's not luck. That's what happens when a prop firm actually pays.

Atlas Funded. Real commissions. Real tracking. Zero BS.

I'm still not stressed about my #Altcoin portfolio.

It's down 55% and I'm planning to be moving my monthly DCA forward to today.

- $NEAR has seen a liquidity sweep and is bouncing back upwards.
- $EIGEN made a higher low and is about to break into a bull market trend.
- $W has made a higher low.

Matter of time until the market picks up momentum again, and if Bitcoin breaks back in its range, altcoins are likely to move fast.
